introHtml: Backend principles cut across every server-side stack. HTTP semantics, REST and GraphQL design, authentication flows (JWT, sessions, OAuth), caching strategies, and the monolith-vs-microservices tradeoff are standard interview topics for any backend or fullstack role.

topics.bodyHtml:
HTTP Versions Comparison

FeatureHTTP/1.1HTTP/2HTTP/3 (QUIC)
MultiplexingNo (pipelining, HOL blocking)Yes (streams)Yes
Header compressionNoHPACKQPACK
TransportTCPTCPUDP (QUIC)
HOL blockingRequest-levelStream-level (TCP HOL remains)No
Server pushNoYesYes
Connection setup1 RTT1 RTT + TLS0-1 RTT (0-RTT resumption)

HTTP Methods

MethodPurposeIdempotentSafeBody
GETRetrieve resourceYesYesNo
POSTCreate resource / submit dataNoNoYes
PUTReplace entire resourceYesNoYes
PATCHPartial updateNo (but can be)NoYes
DELETEDelete resourceYesNoOptional
HEADGET without body (check existence)YesYesNo
OPTIONSCORS preflight, capability checkYesYesNo

Status Codes

CodeMeaningNotes
200OKSuccessful GET/PUT/PATCH
201CreatedSuccessful POST, include Location header
204No ContentSuccessful DELETE, no body
301Moved PermanentlyRedirect, cacheable
302Found (temp redirect)May change method to GET
304Not ModifiedETag/If-None-Match hit
400Bad RequestInvalid input, validation error
401UnauthorizedNot authenticated
403ForbiddenAuthenticated but not authorized
404Not FoundResource doesn't exist
409ConflictDuplicate, version conflict
422Unprocessable EntityValidation failed (semantically invalid)
429Too Many RequestsRate limit hit
500Internal Server ErrorUnexpected server error
502Bad GatewayUpstream server error
503Service UnavailableOverloaded or maintenance

Cookies

Set-Cookie: session=abc123;
  HttpOnly;         /* Cannot be accessed via JS (XSS mitigation) */
  Secure;           /* HTTPS only */
  SameSite=Strict;  /* Never sent cross-site (CSRF mitigation) */
                    /* Lax: sent on top-level navigation GET */
                    /* None: always sent (requires Secure) */
  Domain=.myapp.com;/* Include subdomains */
  Path=/;
  Max-Age=3600;     /* Seconds until expiry (preferred over Expires) */

TLS Handshake (TLS 1.3)

1. Client Hello (supported ciphers, random) → 2. Server Hello (chosen cipher, certificate, random) → 3. Client verifies cert → 4. Key exchange (ECDHE) → 5. Both derive session keys → Encrypted communication. TLS 1.3: 1 RTT (0-RTT for resumption).

REST Principles

  • Uniform Interface: consistent resource-based URLs
  • Stateless: no server-side client state
  • Cacheable: responses can be cached
  • Client-Server: separation of concerns
  • Layered System: intermediaries (LB, cache, gateway)
// Resource naming
GET    /api/v1/users           // list
GET    /api/v1/users/:id       // get one
POST   /api/v1/users           // create
PUT    /api/v1/users/:id       // replace
PATCH  /api/v1/users/:id       // partial update
DELETE /api/v1/users/:id       // delete
GET    /api/v1/users/:id/posts // nested resource

// Versioning strategies:
// 1. URL: /api/v1/... (most common, easy to test)
// 2. Header: Accept: application/vnd.api.v1+json
// 3. Query: /api?version=1

// Cursor-based pagination
// GET /api/posts?limit=20&after=cursor_xyz
// Response: { data: [...], pagination: { nextCursor: "abc", hasMore: true } }

GraphQL

// Schema definition
type User {
  id: ID!
  name: String!
  posts: [Post!]!
}
type Query  { user(id: ID!): User }
type Mutation { createUser(input: CreateUserInput!): User! }

// N+1 Problem: fetching list + N separate queries for relations
// Solution: DataLoader (batching + caching per request)
const userLoader = new DataLoader(async (ids) => {
  const users = await User.findAll({ where: { id: ids } });
  return ids.map(id => users.find(u => u.id === id));
});

Pagination Comparison

TypeHowProsCons
OffsetOFFSET 20 LIMIT 10Simple, random accessInconsistent with inserts/deletes, slow at high offset
CursorWHERE id > cursor LIMIT 10Consistent, fastNo random page jump
KeysetWHERE (col, id) > (val, id)Multi-column sortComplex

OWASP Top 10 (2021)

#VulnerabilityMitigation
A01Broken Access ControlDeny by default, test authorization, log failures
A02Cryptographic FailuresTLS, don't store sensitive data, strong hashing
A03Injection (SQL, NoSQL, command)Parameterized queries, input validation, ORM
A04Insecure DesignThreat modeling, secure design patterns
A05Security MisconfigurationHardened defaults, disable unnecessary features
A06Vulnerable Componentsnpm audit, Dependabot, pin versions
A07Auth & Identification FailuresMFA, strong passwords, session management
A08Software & Data Integrity FailuresVerify checksums, signed packages, CI/CD security
A09Security Logging FailuresLog auth events, failures, alerts
A10SSRFAllowlist URLs, block internal IPs, disable redirects

SQL Injection

// BAD: string interpolation
const query = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '${email}'`;
// email = "' OR '1'='1" returns all users!

// GOOD: parameterized queries
const result = await pool.query(
  "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = $1", [email]
);

XSS Types and CSP

// Stored XSS: script saved in DB, served to users
// Reflected XSS: script in URL parameter, reflected back
// DOM-based XSS: client-side JS manipulates DOM unsafely

// Content Security Policy header
// Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://cdn.com;

// Sanitize user HTML
import DOMPurify from "dompurify";
element.innerHTML = DOMPurify.sanitize(userInput);

CORS

// Preflight (OPTIONS) triggered for: non-simple methods, custom headers
// Server response headers:
// Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://myapp.com
// Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
// Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
// Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
// Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400  (cache preflight 24h)

// Rate limiting algorithms:
// Token bucket: fills at fixed rate, allows bursts up to bucket size
// Leaky bucket: process at fixed rate, smooth output no bursts

// Cache-Control response headers
// Cache-Control: no-store              — Never cache (sensitive data)
// Cache-Control: no-cache              — Revalidate before serving cached
// Cache-Control: private, max-age=300  — Browser only, 5 min
// Cache-Control: public, max-age=86400 — CDN + browser, 1 day
// Cache-Control: public, immutable, max-age=31536000 — versioned assets

// ETags: server sends hash of content
// Client sends: If-None-Match: "etag-value"
// Server: 304 Not Modified if unchanged

// Redis cache-aside pattern
async function getUser(id) {
  const cached = await redis.get(`user:${id}`);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);
  const user = await db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", [id]);
  await redis.setex(`user:${id}`, 3600, JSON.stringify(user));
  return user;
}

// Cache invalidation
await redis.del(`user:${id}`);  // on user update

// Write-through: write to cache AND DB simultaneously
// Write-back: write to cache, async flush to DB (risk of data loss)

RabbitMQ

// Exchange types:
// direct: exact routing key match
// topic:  wildcard routing key (user.* or *.created)
// fanout: broadcast to all bound queues
// headers: match on message headers

channel.publish("user-events", "user.created", Buffer.from(JSON.stringify(user)));

channel.consume("user-notifications", async (msg) => {
  try {
    await processMessage(JSON.parse(msg.content.toString()));
    channel.ack(msg);
  } catch {
    channel.nack(msg, false, true); // requeue
  }
});

Kafka vs RabbitMQ

FeatureKafkaRabbitMQ
ModelLog/pull (consumer reads)Queue/push (broker delivers)
RetentionConfigurable (days/weeks)Until consumed
ReplayYes (seek to offset)No
ThroughputMillions/secThousands/sec
Use caseEvent streaming, audit logTask queues, RPC

AspectMonolithMicroservices
DeploymentSingle unitIndependent services
ScaleScale entire appScale individual services
DevelopmentSimple, single codebaseComplex, polyglot possible
Failure isolationSingle point of failureIsolated failures
Data managementShared DBDB per service
Best forStartups, simple domainsLarge teams, complex domains
// Saga Pattern: distributed transactions across services
// Choreography: services emit events, others react
// Orchestration: central coordinator directs each service

// Outbox Pattern: guarantee message delivery
// 1. Write to DB + outbox table in same transaction
// 2. Background worker reads outbox and publishes to queue
// 3. Delete from outbox on success

// Strangler Fig: gradually replace monolith
// Put proxy in front, route features to new services, strangle old code

// BFF (Backend for Frontend): separate API gateway per client type

keyTakeaways.title: ⚡ Key Takeaways
itemsHtml: HTTP methods have semantic meaning: GET is idempotent and safe; POST is neither; PUT/DELETE are idempotent
REST resource naming uses nouns, not verbs; versioning belongs in the URL (/v1/) or Accept header
JWTs are signed, not encrypted — never put sensitive data in the payload; always verify the signature server-side
Cache-Control headers control both browser and CDN caching; no-store prevents all caching
Message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ) decouple producers from consumers and absorb traffic spikes
Microservices trade deployment independence for distributed systems complexity — choose based on team size and scale
Implement rate limiting at the gateway or load balancer level, not inside each service
